title: Ball of Light
html:
"Will you be fine, Edward?"

I pick up the box and nod at her. Four years of desk clutter in one cardboard box. That's either a statement about my work habits or about the job. I'm not sure which.

The firing happened at 11:14 AM. I know because I was looking at the clock when my head came up off the keyboard and I found my manager standing there with the HR folder already open. One nap, once in four years, and he had the folder ready. A box, a handshake, a security escort to the front door. Not a warning.

I knew the coffee machine on the third floor made a grinding noise on the second cup. I knew which client wanted the report in landscape format and which one would call if it was five minutes late. None of that bought me one conversation before the folder came out.

Outside I wedge the box between my knees and kick the motorbike to life. The ride home is long enough to get angry and run through the anger, and by the time I reach my block it's gone flat. The box digs into my thighs on corners. I let it.

My room is on the third floor of a building that has been losing the argument with humidity since before I moved in. The water stain above the wardrobe has been spreading for three years. I set the box on the floor, sit on the bed, and look at it.

Rent is due in two weeks.

The savings account is not something I open without bracing. The salary was decent but keeping money is a skill I haven't developed. Food, things I convinced myself I needed before payday, food again. Finding a job takes time. I know that now in a way I didn't at 20, when I thought you sent applications and something came back in a week. Most go quiet. The ones that don't run you through three rounds before going quiet anyway.

The novel is the actual reason this happened. Reading past 3 AM because I was at a good part, and by noon the next day my head was on the keyboard. No argument worth making about that. I lie back and look at the ceiling.

A glow comes in at the edge of my vision, warm, where nothing in the room should be warm. I turn my head.

A ball of light hovers above my chest, tennis-ball sized, at the height it would be if I were standing. Nothing in the room explains it.

I sit up and reach toward it. My hand passes through. Zap! Static ran from palm to elbow, sharp enough to pull my hand back. The ball doesn't shift. I look at my palm, nothing visible, tingle fading at the wrist. I try again. Same.

I get up, step around it, and open my laptop.

The thing hovers. I ignore it and search: jobs, admin, data entry, office coordinator. I have a resume. It just hasn't been updated in four years. I open it and sit with it a while. Four years at the same company is either stability or stagnation and right now I don't know how to frame it. The most recent posting wants 3 years' experience and a degree in business administration. I have the years. The degree is close enough. I put in the application.

The ball is still there when I look up.

I close the laptop and eat instant noodles standing over the sink. The ball follows me to the kitchen, keeps its height, doesn't knock the cabinet. I wave my hand through it on the way back. Zap! Same as before. "Okay," I say to it. Nothing happens. I go to sleep.

***

It's still there in the morning, same position above the bed. I walk through it getting up. Zap! I stop caring about the static after the third time.

I spend the morning on the resume. Skills section is thin. Software from the old job, client management, report formatting, cross-departmental coordination. That last one is a generous description of forwarding emails. I put it in anyway. By noon I've sent seven applications. Three auto-reject before I've finished lunch. I do laundry, check email. Two more rejections, one acknowledgement of receipt.

I move a chair so the ball is behind me while I work. It follows me to the laundry room on the second floor. The neighbor across the hall looks at it, looks at me, goes back inside. I don't know what to make of that either.

On the third day the ball moves for the first time. I'm writing a cover letter when it drifts forward and stops between me and the screen. I lean left and it moves left, lean right and it moves right. I put the cover letter down.

My hand goes through it again. Zap! But this time words surface in my head without being a sound first, arriving the way a smell arrives, already there before I've registered them.

---

[Mr. Edward Lee, you have been selected as a Constellation Manager. Please place your palm on the registry to register.]

---

I look at my hand. I look at the ball.

"I have a cover letter to finish," I tell it.

The message stays in my head, unchanged.

The cover letter is for a coordinator position at a logistics company I have no interest in. The ball is apparently offering me a job title with the word Manager in it. I think about the rejection emails and put my palm flat against it.

The static this time is different. A current spreads from the contact point and doesn't stop at the elbow. It keeps going until the light that was just around the ball is everywhere I can see, and the room is gone.

***

Everything around me is white. Flat white in every direction.

---

[Mr. Edward Lee, I am sure you have many questions. Unfortunately, your current permission level does not allow all of them to be answered.]

---

"What is a Constellation Manager?"

---

[In short, a Constellation Manager is a person selected to oversee a constellation of stars. Your current objective is to populate the planets assigned to your constellation and bring them to life.]

---

"Assigned. So I don't have a choice."

---

[Participation is voluntary. Declining returns you to your previous location with no further contact.]

---

I think about the rejection emails. "What do I get out of it?"

---

[Life Energy generated by your constellation can be converted to compensation in your home currency at a rate determined by current conversion tables. Compensation is deposited directly.]

---

"What's the conversion rate?"

---

[Current conversion rate: 1,000 Life Energy equals approximately 3 months of rent at median regional rates.]

---

Rent is due in two weeks. "What's Life Energy?"

---

[Life Energy is the energy produced by living things. The more life exists in your constellation, the more Life Energy accumulates. Life Energy can be spent in the Shop to purchase organisms, terrain modifications, and abilities.]

---

"How much do I have?"

---

[Mr. Edward currently holds a minor fraction of 1 Life Energy. A loan system exists for first-time Constellation Managers. Maximum loan: 1,000 Life Energy.]

---

"Who selected me?"

---

[Permission level too low to answer this question.]

---

"How many planets do I have?"

---

[One planet currently assigned. Designation: Sphere 7-A.]

---

"Can I get more?"

---

[Additional planets may be assigned as constellation performance improves.]

---

---

[Question time limit reached. For further assistance, use the Help function within the Constellation Manager Authority. I wish Mr. Edward a pleasant day.]

---

"I'm not done," I start, and the white is already gone.

Should have asked about the time limit first.

***

The space around me has no color and extends past any visible horizon. There's a floor below me even if I can't see it.

I think about the Authority and a panel opens.

[Enter | Shop | Life | Log | Help | Exit]

I go left to right. Enter tells me I'm already inside. Shop is a long list, organisms at the cheap end, abilities from gods at prices that make 1,000 feel like a starting budget for one decision. Life shows my count at 1,000 borrowed and total constellation life at zero. Log has two entries.

---

[A void of nothingness.]

[Constellation Manager appointed.]

---

Help runs through each function in plain language. I read it twice. Last entry: direct contact with Constellation Manager support, available at any time, cost exactly 1,000 Life Energy.

I close Help. I'm not calling support.

I open the Shop properly. Each item has a generation rate, a cost, conditions it needs. Cheaper entries produce slowly. Higher-output items sit behind conditions that require earlier purchases. Terrain tab: soil modifications, atmosphere adjustments, water seeding, all priced past what I have.

Life, Constellation tab, select the planet.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Barren]

[Rotation period: 28 hours]

[Orbital period: 340 rotations]

[Ambient temperature: -40 to -50 degrees Celsius]

[Atmospheric composition: Trace gases only. Insufficient for respiration.]

[Terrain: Rocky, lifeless. No water detected.]

[Life Energy generated: 0]

---

Minus 40 and no atmosphere. Terrain modifications start at 1,200. I have 1,000.

Water seeding in the Shop costs 800. The description says moisture without atmosphere won't support complex life but begins atmospheric processing that reduces the atmosphere upgrade cost over time. I read it twice to confirm. It does.

800 for water seeding, 200 remaining, which is 40 single-cell organisms at 5 each. The organism description says they can survive in minimal atmosphere if water is present but die in about 4 planetary cycles without more. Buying time, not a solution.

'200 short.'

But the planet generates nothing at zero. The loan sitting idle earns nothing. I open the Shop and stop.

The water seeding is digital. But water is water. I think about the tap water I use to make noodles. I think about the dried-out plant pot by the window, left by the previous tenant. Both came from somewhere. Both have things in them, microscopic things, bacteria, organisms I don't know the names of.

I exit the Authority.

***

The ball of light is near the window. I open the planet panel, which apparently works from my room, and look at the specs again. Rocky, no water, minus 40 degrees.

Water seeding from the Shop just seeds water. What if I put in actual tap water, with whatever's already living in it?

The system said nothing about material from outside the Shop. It said nothing against it either.

I fill a glass from the tap. Open the Authority, navigate to the planet panel. There's no option for external input, just the Shop interface. But when I hold the glass near the panel, no error comes up.

I don't know what "near the panel" means when the planet is however many light-years away. I'm holding a glass of tap water next to a floating interface in my room. It's the stupidest thing I've done this week, and I got fired for sleeping at my desk.

I pour a small amount into my palm and press it against the panel. No confirmation, no balance change.

I check the planet.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Barren]

[Atmospheric composition: Trace gases only. Insufficient for respiration.]

[Terrain: Rocky, lifeless. Trace moisture detected. Origin: unclassified.]

[Life Energy generated: 0]

---

Trace moisture. Origin: unclassified.

'Huh.'

It got there. Whatever was in the tap water, minerals, pipe bacteria, whatever survives municipal treatment, is on Sphere 7-A now. The temperature will freeze it. Probably won't last the day. But it got there for free.

I buy the water seeding for 800, because that comes with the atmospheric processing mechanism and tap water doesn't. Then 40 single-cell organisms for 200.

---

[Water Seeding purchased. Cost: 800 Life Energy. Remaining balance: 200 Life Energy.]

[Sphere 7-A: Water processing initiated. Projected time to minimal atmospheric development: 12 planetary rotations.]

[Single-cell organisms (x40) purchased. Cost: 200 Life Energy. Remaining balance: 0 Life Energy.]

[Organisms placed. Survival rate in current conditions: low. Monitor atmospheric progress.]

---

12 rotations at 28 hours. 14 days. Rent is due in 14 days.

I close the panel. Balance at zero, planet has water and 40 organisms that probably won't survive the week, and I still had a cover letter to finish. I send it, add three more applications, and by the time my eyes get heavy I've got an interview tomorrow. Data entry, insurance company, other side of the city. Not good, but it's something.

The ball dimmed somewhere before I fell asleep. I didn't notice exactly when.
